Even if White decides to grab your d6 pawn with a check, your king can safely step up to h5, and your highly active rook ensures you maintain excellent counterplay.

Takeaway: In endgames, prioritize active piece play and creating immediate threats over slow, defensive pawn moves.

I'd play cxb4. Before you rush to castle, always look for pieces you can simply win — here the black bishop on b4 is undefended and your pawn on c3 can just grab it for free. Castling was reasonable for king safety, but it lets Black save that hanging bishop; taking material first is almost always the priority when the capture is safe. After cxb4 you're up a whole piece and can castle next move anyway.

Takeaway: Before making a "safe" move, scan for any enemy piece you can capture for free.

I'd play cxb4. Before castling, take the undefended bishop on b4 with your c-pawn; that also attacks the queen on a5. This is a very beginner-friendly habit: when your opponent has a loose piece, deal with it before making a normal developing or safety move.  
Takeaway: Before castling, always check whether you can win an undefended piece right now.

I'd play cxb4. Notice that your bishop on b4 was already under attack by your pawn on c3, and instead of dealing with that you castled and gave the piece away for free. I'd play O-O. Your Be3 develops a piece, but in this sharp position your king is still in the center and Black already has active pieces pointing toward it. I'd play a6. Your castling move missed a simple chance to gain time: a6 attacks the queen on b5 and makes White spend a move responding. I'd play a6. Castling here ignored the fact that your opponent's queen sits deep in your territory on b5 — before doing anything else, you want to challenge that intruder and gain time. I'd play Ra3. This is more active than f6: instead of just blocking, you attack the knight on f3 and make White spend a move dealing with your rook. If White grabs the d-pawn with Rxd6+, you still have f6 to block the check, while your rook remains active and annoying on the third rank.

I'd play Ra4. Your Kf6 move is solid in spirit because it defends, but here the more active rook move is stronger: Ra4 attacks both the a7 pawn and the g4 pawn. I'd play Nd7. 

Castling kingside right away walks into a structural trap: White can simply take your knight with their bishop, forcing you to recapture with your g-pawn and completely shattering the pawn shield right in front of your king. I'd play Nd7. 

Castling short seems like the natural way to get your king to safety, but it walks into a trap here. I'd play Rxf7. Takeaway: In pawn races, deal with the promoting pawn first—active rook play beats passive king moves.

I'd play Ra4. I'd play a6. I'd play Ra4.

While stepping your king to f6 is a natural instinct to improve its position, it doesn't address White's only real counterplay: the dangerous passed pawn on a7. I'd play Bc6+. Your Bxa6 grabbed an undefended knight, but Bc6+ is more forcing: it checks the king while also attacking the rook on a8. I'd play Ra4. Your Kf6 is natural, but it gives White time to move the rook and make the a-pawn a real problem; Ra4 deals with the most urgent issue first. I'd play Ra4.

I'd play gxg5. You already have a bishop sitting deep in Black's camp on b7, so there's no rush to trade it off for the knight on a6 — that just swaps your active piece for one stuck on the rim. I'd play Bc6+. Your bishop grabbed the knight on a6, but the real prize here is the undefended rook in the corner — and with check, you can win it while your opponent has no time to save it. I'd play Kf4. Your f6 move rushes the pawn forward, but Kf4 first improves the king and defends the pawn on f5. A simple plan is Kf4 Kf6 Ke3, keeping your king active instead of committing the pawn too soon. In this kind of endgame, king activity and pawn support matter more than speed.

I'd play Kxd5. Your move f6 pushed the pawn where it can't be defended, and after the black king simply grabs it your passed pawn disappears — leaving you nothing to play for. I'd play Kf4. This is a tough spot where you're fighting for a draw, so your top job is to keep your one asset — the f-pawn — protected while staying active. I'd play Bc6+.

I'd play Kf4. Your king is in check from the rook on a5, so you must move it! I'd play Rxh8. 

You actually found the only logical move in a very difficult position. Even though you are down material, you absolutely have to eliminate White's most dangerous piece, the queen. I'd play Kf4.

I'd play gxh6. That bishop on h6 is attacking your rook on f8 and sitting right next to your king, so the simplest, strongest reply is just to capture it and win a whole piece for free. I'd play Kd6. Instead of rushing the pawn with f6, put your king where it makes Black’s own d-pawn block the rook from attacking your f-pawn. I'd play gxh6. The bishop sitting on h6 is simply hanging, and your pawn on g7 can capture it for free — grabbing a whole piece for nothing is a great deal! I'd play h5. Your move f3 pushed the pawn away from support and let White's bishop slide to h3, so your f4-pawn actually became weaker instead of stronger. I'd play h5. Playing f3 looks tempting because it attacks the bishop, but it actually helps White by letting the bishop slide to h3 where it becomes much more active. I'd play Qc2.

I'd play exd6. I'd play Nf3. This develops your last knight while also defending both central pawns on d4 and e5, so it improves your position without creating new weaknesses. I'd play Nf3. Your h3 move is understandable — you wanted to deal with that advanced pawn on g3 — but it's passive and lets Black off the hook while your king still sits in the center. I'd play Nf3.

I'd play Qxg2+. This keeps the initiative by using a check first, and it also attacks the rook on e2 and the knight on f3. I'd play Ne2. 

Instead of spending time on a prophylactic pawn move like h3, it is critical to prioritize your kingside development to get your king to safety. Developing the knight to e2 prepares castling while immediately bringing another piece into the game to help blockade or challenge Black's intrusive pawns. In such sharp, unbalanced positions, accelerating your development is much more reliable than poking at the opponent's structure prematurely. 

Takeaway: Always prioritize piece development and king safety over slow pawn moves when your king is still stuck in the center.

I'd play Bxf3. Your move was actually the right one! By capturing on f3, you remove the knight that defends key squares and open lines toward White's king, and if White recaptures with the pawn (gxf3), your queen and the pin along the g-file give you strong pressure — for example Qxf3 hits the rook on e2 while keeping the attack alive. I'd play Qxg2+. Takeaway: When pieces are hanging, look for a forcing check first — it can rescue the situation before you give anything up.

I'd play b3. This is more forcing than f3: instead of chasing the bishop and letting White simply take the pawn, you push the passed pawn with queen support and make White deal with it right away. If White tries Rxb3, then Qxb3 wins the rook, so your plan is simple: keep the b-pawn alive and push it toward promotion.

I'd play Rxe2.

I'd play Rc8. 

Your move Bxf3 grabs a knight but overlooks a critical, game-ending threat: your rook on e8 is undefended and heavily attacked. If you ignore it, White simply plays Rxe8+. By sliding the rook to safety with Rc8, you avoid disaster entirely. Even though White can follow up by taking your bishop on h5, your overall position remains overwhelmingly strong because your major pieces are secure.

I'd play f5+. This gives check while using the f-pawn, so you keep your h-pawn safer than in the game idea of h5+ Kxh5. After f5+ Kh5, your pawns are still working together and you can keep pushing for space instead of losing one right away. Takeaway: When you have two checking pawn moves, prefer the one that keeps more pawns alive and connected.

I'd play f5+. Your instinct to give check was good, but h5+ lets the enemy king slip forward and get active, while f5+ pushes the king back and keeps you in control. The bigger picture here is that you're down material with an exposed king of your own, so instead of chasing checks that go nowhere, aim to trade off pawns and steer toward a drawn ending where the extra queen can't easily win. Giving a check that improves *your* position rather than the opponent's is the key difference.

Takeaway: When you're worse, prefer checks that push the enemy king backward, not ones that let it march toward your side.

I'd play hxg3. Your h3 move spent a tempo without removing the pawn that was already deep in your position; hxg3 directly challenges it and reduces Black’s attacking chances. If Black replies ...fxg3, that pawn has at least been pulled away from f4, and your next plan is simple: develop Nf3 and get your king safe.

I'd play f5+. Your instinct to check the king was good, but on h5 the black king can simply capture your pawn, so the check gives away material for nothing. With f5+ you instead push the king back and keep your pawns working together — after the king steps aside you can follow with g6, using your pawns to make real progress rather than trading one off.

Takeaway: Before giving a check, make sure the checking piece can't just be captured for free.

I'd play f5+. 

In a desperate situation where your opponent has a Queen and you only have pawns, your only practical chance is to push your pawns as aggressively as possible to create threats. Pushing f5+ directly challenges the Black king and prepares to push your other pawns forward to create a dangerous passed pawn. While your move h5+ also gave a check, it allowed Black's king to safely capture the pawn without letting you create as much chaos.

Takeaway: When playing a desperately lost endgame, push your pawns aggressively to create the maximum amount of practical trouble for your opponent.

I'd play a5. 

In positions where you are significantly down in material, your main goal should be to avoid giving away your remaining pawns for free. Your move h5+ allows Black's King to simply capture the pawn, making your defensive task even harder. Playing a5 safely advances your pawn on the other side of the board and forces Black to work for the win rather than handing it to them.

Takeaway: Always make sure your checks and pawn pushes don't just give away material for free, especially when you are already defending.

I'd play f5+. This check uses your king and pawns together: the f-pawn is protected by your king, and it pushes the black king back to g7 while keeping your kingside pawns connected. Your h5+ idea also checks, but it rushes the outside pawn forward before improving the pawn chain; start by making the protected passer and then keep advancing with support.

Takeaway: In pawn endings, push the pawn that stays protected and improves your whole structure.

I'd play Bxh4. Instead of Rxc3, choose the forcing move that removes White’s knight and also puts pressure on f2. This is easier to trust: when there is a loose-looking pawn to grab, first check whether you can improve your position by taking a more important piece with a clear follow-up target.  
Takeaway: Prefer forcing moves that win or remove active pieces before grabbing pawns.

I'd play Qxd3. Your Rxc3 grabbed a pawn but left your queen sitting on b1 while both of White's rooks stayed active — you never dealt with the real problem, which is that White's pieces are coordinating dangerously against your king. Instead, notice that your queen on b1 already guards the d3 rook's target square, so you can simply capture the rook on d3: after Qxd3, White recaptures with Qxd3 and then Rxd3, and you've traded down into a safe, roughly level position with your king out of danger. That's a much cleaner outcome than clinging to an extra pawn while your king stays exposed.

Takeaway: When you're under pressure, look first for a clean trade that removes the enemy's active pieces before you go hunting for pawns.

I'd play Bxh4. Grabbing on c3 with the rook was tempting because it's a free-looking pawn, but it ignored what was happening around your own king and pieces — never grab material until you've checked what the opponent's active pieces can do in return. Bxh4 removes White's dangerous knight, which was eyeing jumps toward your king, and it does so cleanly; after White recaptures with the queen, you've traded off an attacker and can settle your defense with a move like Qe1. That's a much healthier way to fight than opening lines near your king.

Takeaway: Before capturing a pawn, first neutralize the opponent's most active attacking piece.

I'd play Bf6. Your move Nxd1 went for the undefended rook right away, but Bf6 first improves your bishop while also defending the queen on e7. The concrete plan is simple: after White responds, you are ready to attack the pawn on b2 instead of letting your queen remain loose. This is a more controlled way to keep pressure without rushing into a capture.

Takeaway: Before grabbing an undefended piece, check whether one of your important pieces needs protection first.

I'd play Bxf3. This is a good forcing capture because the knight on f3 is the key defender and also blocks your queen’s pressure; if White recaptures, your queen can take back on f3 with check. So the plan is simple: remove the blocker first, then use the active queen to keep White’s king under pressure rather than just trading pieces randomly.  
Takeaway: When your queen is active near the king, look for the defender or blocker you can remove first.

I'd play Qxe6. Grabbing the loose knight on e6 with your queen is decisive because that knight was the key attacker near your king, and taking it wins a whole piece while keeping your king safe. Your move Nxd1 was tempting—winning the rook—but it ignores that White's pieces are swarming your king; after Nxd1 you let White keep the dangerous e6-knight and open attacking lines instead of removing the threat and staying up material. Snapping off the strongest enemy piece near your king both defuses the attack and cashes in cleanly.

Takeaway: Before grabbing material on the far side of the board, first eliminate the enemy piece that's actually attacking your king.

I'd play Qc1. Grabbing the pawn with your rook on c3 unfortunately throws away material and exposes your position. Instead, bringing your queen to c1 targets the exact same c3 pawn safely, while also keeping an eye on your kingside defenses. This way, you keep up the pressure on White's queenside pawns without giving away a full piece.

Takeaway: Always look for ways to attack weaknesses with your long-range pieces before committing to a risky capture.

I'd play Bxh4.

Taking the knight simplifies the position and removes a tricky piece that could otherwise jump into dangerous squares around your king. While grabbing the c3 pawn with your rook is tempting, it opens up lines for White's rooks to coordinate and create immediate threats, especially since White already has a powerful rook on the seventh rank. By trading first, you reduce White's attacking potential and keep your position solid.

Takeaway: When your opponent has active pieces near your king, trading them off is often safer and more practical than grabbing a minor pawn.

I'd play Bf6. Grabbing the rook with Nxd1 looks like the greedy choice, but it walks straight into trouble because that knight was doing important defensive work — capturing pulls it away and your position around the king loosens dangerously. Bf6 is far stronger: it keeps everything coordinated, adds a defender to your queen on e7, and takes aim at b2 right next to White's king. After something like Rhe1, you can crash in with Bxb2+ and turn your pieces toward the enemy king rather than chasing material.

Takeaway: Before you snatch a hanging piece, ask what that piece was defending — a well-placed attacking move often beats a greedy capture.

I'd play Bf6.

Grabbing the exchange with Nxd1 is tempting, but it releases the immense pressure you have built up. By playing Bf6, you bring your bishop into a dominating diagonal where it eyes the vulnerable b2 pawn, setting up a crushing attack on the white king. It also adds a layer of defense to your queen, keeping your pieces coordinated for a decisive blow rather than settling for a small material gain.

Takeaway: When you have a strong attack brewing, look for ways to activate your remaining pieces and create mating threats rather than cashing in too early for an exchange.

I'd play Bxh4. This uses the important idea your move missed: White’s queen is overloaded, because it is tied to both the knight on h4 and the rook on d3. If White answers Qxh4, then Qxd3 wins the rook, so you win more than just a pawn and keep the attack going. Rxc3 grabs material, but Bxh4 targets the loose pieces in the right order.

Takeaway: When several enemy pieces are loose, look for the capture that overloads their defender.

I'd play Nf3. It develops a minor piece while also defending both your pawn on d4 and your bishop on g5, which was undefended. Instead of pushing the d-pawn too soon with d5, first make your pieces and pawns more secure so your position is easier to play. Takeaway: Before making a pawn break, check whether you can improve a piece while defending something important.

I'd play Bxf6. Your move d5 tried to open the center, but it lets Black keep everything together and doesn't grab any real advantage. Instead, trading with Bxf6 shatters the pawns in front of Black's king — after ...Qxf6 (or ...gxf6) his kingside gets weakened, and with your bishop and queen already eyeing that side you'll have targets to aim at. This is a clean, forcing idea: when you see your bishop can capture a defending knight near the enemy king, it's often worth grabbing it.

Takeaway: Trading off a knight that guards the enemy king can crack open his shelter for your attack.

I'd play Bxf6. Your bishop on g5 is being attacked by the h-pawn, so before making a center move like d5, save it or trade it for something valuable. Bxf6 is simple and healthy: you trade your attacked bishop for Black’s knight, and if Black recaptures with Qxf6, you can continue developing calmly. Takeaway: When your piece is attacked, first ask whether you can trade it for an equal or better piece.

I'd play Bxf6. Your move d5 pushed a pawn forward but ignored the fact that your bishop on g5 was hanging — Black could just take it. Instead, notice that your bishop is already attacking the knight on f6, so capture it first: after Bxf6 Black recaptures with the queen, and you've traded off cleanly while keeping everything safe. Always deal with your own attacked pieces before starting new plans.

Takeaway: When one of your pieces is under attack, check whether it can grab something useful before it gets taken for free.

I'd play Bxg5. 

While grabbing the exchange with your knight seems tempting, capturing the queen directly eliminates White's most dangerous attacking piece and simplifies the position in your favor. When you are already winning materially or positionally, removing the opponent's best counterplay is usually the safest path to victory.

Takeaway: Always prioritize eliminating the opponent's most active pieces when simplifying a winning position.

I'd play b4. Instead of using Bb3 just to defend the pawn on d5, b4 asks Black a direct question and attacks the pawn on c5. A clear plan is b4 cxb4 axb4, opening play on the queenside while keeping your position active. At your level, this kind of move is important: when you have a strong advanced pawn, look for ways to support it by gaining space, not only by defending it.

Takeaway: When a move only defends, ask whether you have an active pawn break that improves your position instead.

I'd play Bxf6. 

Black is attacking your bishop with their pawn on h6. When a minor piece is attacked by a pawn, you generally need to move it to safety or trade it away, because ignoring it will cost you the piece. Trading your bishop for the knight on f6 is the simplest way to handle the threat while keeping your position solid, allowing you to focus on developing your remaining pieces. Your move d5 completely ignores the danger to your bishop and gives away a pawn for free. 

Takeaway: When your piece is attacked by a pawn, always resolve that threat before trying to force the action somewhere else.

I'd play Nb5. Your Bb3 was a passive retreat that leaves Black's strong knight on e5 unchallenged and does nothing about the center; instead, Nb5 immediately targets the dark squares and the weak c7 point while eyeing the d6 outpost. After something like Nb5 Rad8, you've grabbed the initiative and forced Black to react to your ideas rather than sitting back and letting that beautiful e5 knight dominate. The whole point is that when you have a space edge with the d5 pawn, you want your pieces jumping into Black's camp, not shuffling back to b3.

Takeaway: When you hold a space advantage, look for active outposts and infiltration squares rather than retreating your pieces to safety.

I'd play Bxf6. Your bishop on g5 was under attack by the pawn on h6, and pushing your pawn to d5 ignores that threat, allowing Black to simply capture your bishop for free. By taking the knight on f6, you safely resolve the danger while trading off one of Black's key defenders. Always make sure to check if your opponent is attacking your pieces before continuing with your own offensive plans.

Takeaway: Always deal with direct threats to your pieces before launching new attacks.

I'd play b4. Your Bb3 was passive — it just tucks the bishop away to defend d5, but the pawn wasn't really the problem; your real task in this position is to break open the queenside where your pawns and pieces have room to work. With b4 you strike directly at c5, and after b4 cxb4 axb4 you open the a-file and create pressure against Black's queenside pawns while your rook and knight get active lines. That's a concrete, thematic plan that beats sitting back and shuffling a piece to a defensive square.

Takeaway: When your position calls for initiative, look for the pawn break that opens files for your pieces rather than a passive defensive retreat.

I'd play Nf4. This move uses the knight actively: it attacks the pawns on d3, g2, and h3 all at once. Your Ndb4 move attacked a2 and d3 and defended the other knight, but Nf4 creates more pressure on important kingside pawns while still keeping the initiative. Look for moves that make one piece create several problems for your opponent.

Takeaway: When you can, improve a piece to a square where it attacks multiple targets.

I'd play b4. 

While Bb3 defends your d5 pawn, it's a bit slow and doesn't create any new problems for Black. By playing b4, you immediately challenge Black's pawn structure and fight for space on the queenside. This pawn break forces Black to make a decision about the tension, and if they capture with cxb4, you can recapture with axb4 and open the a-file for your own counterplay. 

Takeaway: In cramped or difficult positions, actively look for pawn breaks to challenge your opponent's structure and open lines for your pieces.

I'd play Nf4. Your knight on d5 was sitting a bit exposed, so moving it was the right instinct — but Ndb4 sends it to the edge where it does little and can be nudged back. Instead, Nf4 jumps the knight to a strong, active square where it eyes several of White's pawns at once, keeping your piece busy and creating real pressure rather than just running from a threat.

Takeaway: When you move a piece out of danger, choose a square where it attacks something, not just a safe corner.

I'd play Nf4. Your move Ndb4 sent a knight to the edge where it doesn't threaten anything and can be brushed away, while Nf4 plants a knight on a powerful central square right next to White's king. From f4 the knight eyes weak squares and supports your kingside pawns rolling forward with ideas like ...g5 and ...f4, giving you a real attacking plan instead of just shuffling pieces around.

Takeaway: Put knights on strong central squares near the enemy king, not on the rim where they achieve nothing.

I'd play Qe2. 

While moving your bishop to b3 feels active, it actually sidelines the piece and doesn't address the main tension in the center. By playing Qe2, you support your central e4 pawn and prepare to challenge Black's strongly placed knight on e5. It also steps off the back rank, helping to coordinate your heavy pieces for the middlegame battle. 

Takeaway: Always prioritize moves that improve your piece coordination and fight for central control over single-piece maneuvers on the flank.

I'd play Nf4. 

Your knight was under attack, so moving it was definitely the right idea. However, dropping it on b4 puts it on the edge of the board where it has less influence. Jumping to f4 is much more active: it saves the knight, attacks the d3 pawn, and starts putting uncomfortable pressure near White's king. 

Takeaway: When you have to retreat or save an attacked piece, look for the square that creates the most new problems for your opponent.

I'd play Kb8. 

When you castle queenside, your king often lands on the slightly exposed c-file, leaving your a7 pawn undefended. Tucking your king safely into the corner with Kb8 is a classic, essential improving move that secures your position. Your move, Ndb4, takes your beautifully centralized knight on d5 and moves it to the edge of the board where it doesn't create any immediate threats. It's usually better to maintain your strong central pieces and focus on king safety.

Takeaway: After castling queenside, sliding your king to b8 is a standard, highly useful prophylactic move to keep your king safe and defend your pawns.

I'd play Kd5. This is the most natural way to keep your king active and ready to head toward c6 after Kf5. Compared with Kd6, it keeps the plan simple: improve the king first, then keep moving it toward the queenside. You don’t need to rush—just choose the king move that gives you the clearest next step.

Takeaway: In king-and-pawn endings, pick the king move that keeps your next route clear and purposeful.

I'd play Kd5. In king-and-pawn races like this, your job is to shepherd your b-pawn to promotion while keeping the enemy king cut off from it, and Kd5 does both — it defends the pawn's path and keeps guarding the queening square. The trouble with Kd6 is that it drifts your king away from the action and lets Black's king edge closer to your b-pawn; you want your king in front of or beside your passed pawn, not wandering. From d5 you can support the pawn's march up the board while your opponent's a-pawn and h-pawn can't run fast enough to matter.

Takeaway: In pawn races, use your king to escort your own passed pawn rather than chasing the opponent's.

I'd play Kd5. This is a race, and every step your king takes toward the queenside matters: you need to shepherd your b-pawn forward while getting close enough to stop Black's h-pawn if needed. The key idea is that after Kd5, if Black plays Kf5 you continue Kc6, heading straight for the a6-pawn and clearing the way for your own pawn to run. Your Kd6 wasn't a disaster in plan, but Kd5 keeps your king one useful step more central and flexible, staying closer to both the action and Black's advancing king.

Takeaway: In king-and-pawn races, march your king by the shortest route toward your promotion side while staying close enough to contest the enemy pawn.

I'd play Nf4. This keeps the knight active but aims it toward White’s king instead of drifting to the edge with Ndb4. From f4, the knight attacks d3 and g2, so your concrete plan is to increase pressure on White’s weak dark squares and make their king feel unsafe.  
Takeaway: When you have an active knight, improve it toward the enemy king and weak pawns, not toward the rim.

I'd play Rd1. Bb3 puts the bishop on a diagonal your own d5-pawn blocks, so it looks active but doesn’t really increase your pressure. Rd1 is more purposeful: it backs up your strongest asset, the passed d-pawn, and prepares to push d6 under better conditions while keeping your pieces coordinated. Takeaway: Improve the piece that supports your main pawn break before making “active-looking” moves.

I'd play Kd5. 

In this difficult endgame, Black's passed h-pawn is extremely dangerous and our king is too far away to stop it. Since we can't catch the pawn, our best practical try is to sprint to the queenside as fast as possible. By stepping diagonally to d5, we take the most direct route toward c6 to target Black's a-pawn and try to create our own passed pawn. 

Takeaway: When you cannot stop an enemy passed pawn, race your king to the opposite side of the board to hunt their remaining pawns and create counter-threats.

I'd play Nf2. This is a cleaner version of the idea behind Nxd1: instead of grabbing one rook right away, you put a protected knight on f2 where it forks both rooks and also hits the knight on h3. If White tries 2.Nxf2, then Bxf2 keeps the pressure and removes a defender, so your pieces stay active rather than just cashing in too early.  
Takeaway: When you have a strong knight fork, look for the square that creates the most threats while staying protected.

I'd play b5. 

In a pure pawn race where your king cannot catch the opponent's passed pawn, your only chance is to create a dangerous passed pawn of your own as quickly as possible. By playing your king to d6, you lost a crucial tempo. Pushing the pawn immediately forces Black into a losing footrace; if they capture your pawn, your king can quickly step in to support a new passed pawn, and if they push past, your pawn will safely promote first.

Takeaway: When you can't stop the opponent's passed pawn, focus entirely on pushing your own pawns as fast as possible.

I'd play Nxg4. Your Nxc6 gave up your strong, centralized knight and, after ...bxc6, actually reinforced Black's pawn center instead of pressuring it. The cleaner idea is to first remove the annoying bishop on g4 with your knight — this also hits the f6 knight and keeps your e5 knight's tension for a moment; after ...Nxg4, dxc5 opens things up favorably for you. The key habit is to resolve tension by trading off Black's active pieces rather than voluntarily surrendering your own best-placed piece.

Takeaway: Before you trade, ask which piece is doing more work — don't hand over your strongest piece to fix a lesser one.

I'd play Nxf7. Your Nxc6 released the tension and handed Black an easy, comfortable game after ...bxc6, since you traded off a strong centralized knight for nothing and let his pawns get repaired. Instead, notice that f7 is only guarded by the king: after Nxf7 Rxf7, you have Bxc6 winning material back with a continuing initiative against his loosened kingside and the pinned pieces on the c-file. The lesson is that before you make a routine capture, check whether a more forcing option—an undefended square next to the enemy king—changes the whole evaluation.

Takeaway: When a piece sits on a square defended only by the king, always calculate the sacrifice before settling for a quiet trade.

I'd play Bxc6. This fixes the key choice in your Nxc6 idea: if you want to remove the knight on c6, let the bishop do it, so your knight on e5 stays available for Nxg4. It also gives your queen on a4 extra support while putting pressure on the pawns on b7 and d5.  
Takeaway: When two pieces can make a capture, choose the one that keeps your more active piece available for the next job.

I'd play dxe5. Instead of trading with Bxd2+, Black should first remove White’s pawn on e5, which is a central pawn that is available to capture. This also puts pressure on White’s pawn on d4, giving Black a simple follow-up plan: challenge White’s center before going for side trades.  

Takeaway: When your opponent has advanced central pawns, look first for safe captures that break up their center.

I'd play Nxg4.

Trading your powerful, centralized knight for the one on c6 helps Black consolidate their center and opens the b-file for their rook. Instead, grabbing the bishop with Nxg4 secures the bishop pair for you and removes Black's most active minor piece. By taking the bishop, you improve your long-term prospects without giving up your active outpost for nothing.

Takeaway: Before trading away a well-placed piece, make sure you are getting a tangible benefit in return, such as the bishop pair or a structural advantage.

I'd play dxe5. Before trading pieces, look at the center: White has a pawn on e5 that's attacking your knight on c6, and you can simply capture it to win a pawn and open things up. Trading your bishop with Bxd2+ actually helps White by developing their king toward safety and clearing their back rank, so you gave up an active bishop for nothing while ignoring the free pawn right in front of you.

Takeaway: Before making a trade, check whether you can just win material with a capture instead.

I'd play dxe5. Your bishop on b4 was hanging, but trading it off with Bxd2+ actually helped White by developing his queen or king toward the center for free — and it wasn't the move the position was asking for. Instead, notice that White's pawn on e5 is undefended, so you can simply grab it with dxe5, winning a healthy pawn and also striking at White's pawn on d4. Before trading pieces, always ask whether there's a free pawn or a better plan sitting right in front of you.

Takeaway: Look for undefended enemy pawns before rushing into a trade — a free pawn is often the best move on the board.

I'd play Ndf3. 

By playing Nxc6, you voluntarily traded away your most active piece (the knight on e5) and released the tension too early. After Black recaptures with their b-pawn, they strengthen their center and open the b-file for their rook, giving them easy queenside counterplay. Instead, Ndf3 simply overprotects your powerful e5 outpost and brings another piece into the game, keeping Black under pressure while you prepare to castle. 

Takeaway: Don't rush to trade off your best-placed pieces; maintain the tension to keep your opponent restricted.

I'd play Nxd5.

Trading your bishop on d2 isn't terrible, but it brings another White piece into the game and leaves that annoying, centralized knight alive on d5. By playing Nxd5, you immediately eliminate White's best-placed piece. After White recaptures, you've simplified the position and removed a major headache from the center of the board.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a powerful knight parked on an outpost in your half of the board, trading it off is usually a top priority.

I'd play dxe5.

Trading your active bishop with Bxd2+ just helps White develop their pieces and doesn't address the main issue in the position: White's strong center. White's e5 pawn is cramping your position and is currently undefended. By playing dxe5, you eliminate this annoying central pawn, open up lines for your own pieces, and start fighting back for control of the middle of the board. 

Takeaway: When your opponent pushes a pawn far into your territory without enough support, look for ways to capture it and challenge their central control.

I'd play Ke5. Your Kd6 heads toward the queenside, but it gives Black’s h-pawn the tempo it needs to run; Ke5 keeps your king close enough to stop it. If Black tries 1...h3, then 2.Ke4 puts your king on the right path to catch the pawn before it becomes dangerous. Think of this as choosing the urgent job first: stop the outside passer, then worry about the queenside pawns.  
Takeaway: In king-and-pawn endings, chase the pawn that can queen fastest before grabbing distant material.

I'd play Nb3. Your move c4 tries to grab space, but it actually locks your own bishop's diagonal and hands Black the b4 square while doing nothing about his queenside pawns rolling forward. Instead, Nb3 immediately targets the c5 and a5 pawns, pressuring Black's advanced pawns and preparing to trade off or win one of them while keeping your pieces active. A natural follow-up is that Black must spend time defending c5, letting you improve with pieces rather than committing pawns you can't take back.

Takeaway: Before pushing a pawn, check whether a piece move can pressure your opponent's weaknesses without creating new ones of your own.

I'd play a4. Your move c4 tried to grab space on the queenside, but it actually locked things up and released the tension in a way that helped Black; instead, a4 strikes directly at the b5 pawn and pries open the queenside where Black is a bit loose. A natural follow-up is that if Black defends with Ba6, you can just capture on b5, winning a pawn or forcing concessions. The idea is to play where you can create immediate, concrete pressure rather than making a slow committal push.

Takeaway: Before pushing a pawn to gain space, check whether a more active pawn break attacks something and keeps the initiative.

I'd play O-O. Your Nxc6 trades away a strong centralized knight and helps Black untangle, while castling first keeps that pressure and fixes White’s biggest practical issue: the king is still in the center. After O-O, your plan is simple and useful: Re1, h3 to question the bishop, and only then decide whether Nxc6 is actually worth playing.  
Takeaway: When you have a strong outpost, don’t trade it away unless you gain something concrete.

I'd play Re1. This is a calm improving move: the rook defends your queen on d1 and reinforces the pawn on e4, so your position becomes easier to handle. Compared with c4, it improves coordination first instead of making another pawn commitment; after that, a3 is a simple follow-up to keep building steadily. Takeaway: Before pushing a new pawn, look for a move that improves a piece while defending something important.

I'd play a4. 

Playing c4 was a bit rigid, locking up the pawn structure in a way that doesn't really challenge Black's setup. Instead, striking back with a4 immediately attacks the b5 pawn and forces Black to make a difficult decision about their expanding queenside. By breaking the tension on your own terms, you prevent Black from slowly cramping your pieces. 

Takeaway: When your opponent starts marching pawns down the board, look for pawn breaks to challenge their structure before it becomes overwhelming.

I'd play dxe5. This calmly takes away White’s advanced center pawn and keeps your bishop on b4 active instead of trading it off right away. If White grabs the bishop with Nxb4, you can answer Nxb4, and your knight lands with tempo on the queen. Takeaway: When your piece is attacked, look for a useful counter in the center before automatically trading it away.

I'd play a4. 

Playing c4 is a bit rigid and allows Black to either lock down the queenside or trade pawns on their own terms. Instead, playing a4 immediately strikes at Black's advanced queenside pawn chain. By challenging the b5 pawn directly, you force Black to make a difficult decision, creating weaknesses in their structure and opening up lines for your rook. 

Takeaway: When your opponent pushes pawns aggressively on one side of the board, use pawn breaks to challenge their structure before they cramp your position.

I'd play Kf1. It solves the same main issue as your Kf2 by attacking the knight on e2 and defending g2, but it keeps the king a bit less committed. A natural continuation is Kf1 Nxg3+ hxg3, and White has at least cleaned up the knight while keeping the kingside connected. Your Kf2 was playable, but at this level look for the king move that gains time without stepping farther into the open.

Takeaway: When moving the king in a tense position, prefer the square that solves the problem while staying as safe and flexible as possible.

I'd play a4. Your c4 move hits b5, but it also gives up control of d4 and locks your own bishop into a more fixed structure. With a4, you challenge Black’s advanced queenside pawns from the side: if ...b4, cxb4 opens lines, and if Black ignores it, axb5 can start breaking up those pawns.  
Takeaway: When your opponent’s pawns are advanced, attack the chain from the side before locking the center.

I'd play Rxe2. The knight on e2 is a loose piece attacking near your king, and after Rxe2 Qxe2 you win it back cleanly with Bd5+ — your bishop hits the king on the long diagonal and the queen at the same time, so you emerge up material. Kf2 walked toward the trouble instead of resolving it, leaving that active knight in your camp and your king exposed to the rolling f- and g-pawns; grabbing the knight first eliminates the threat and hands you the initiative through the check.

Takeaway: Before shuffling your king to safety, look for a capture or in-between check that removes the attacker entirely.

I'd play Kf1. The knight on e2 is a live problem, and both king moves that attack it make sense, but Kf1 is the cleaner square: on f2 your king walks into the pin-and-shove idea after ...f4, hitting your bishop on g3 while your king sits exposed on the same file. From f1 you sidestep that f-file pressure, keep the pressure on the e2-knight, and if Black grabs with ...Nxg3+ you simply recapture with the h-pawn and stay solid. The key correction is that your king wants to get off the f-file where Black's ...f4 break gains time.

Takeaway: When choosing between similar king moves, pick the square that avoids your opponent's pawn breaks and gaining-tempo threats.

I'd play Qxb3. Your move Be6 was a natural developing idea, but it overlooks that your queen and White's queen are staring at each other with nothing in between — you can simply grab the queen for free! Trading queens like this wins a whole piece worth of material, since White recaptures with the pawn (axb3) and you come out clearly ahead. Always pause before developing to check whether one of your pieces is already attacking something valuable.

Takeaway: Before making a quiet move, scan for pieces you can capture right now — free material beats slow development.

I'd play Kf1.

When dealing with a knight check, stepping to a square that forces the enemy piece to make a decision is usually your best bet. By playing Kf1, you attack the pesky knight on e2, forcing it to immediately resolve the tension—likely by trading for your bishop on g3. Stepping to f2 is an inaccuracy because it walks right into Black's pawn storm, allowing them to push f4 to attack your bishop and gain dangerous momentum.

In these pawn endings, activity often matters more than simply guarding one loose pawn.

Takeaway: In king-and-pawn endings, prefer the active king move that creates pressure while defending something important.

I'd play f5. In a king-and-pawn ending like this, you want to create threats and open lines rather than just shuffle your king; Kc3 lets Black's king stay active on b5 and does nothing about the kingside where the game will actually be decided. After f5, if Black takes with gxf5 you can answer exf6 with a passer, and if Black plays exf5 the e5-pawn becomes a dangerous runner while your king can support it. The point is to fix or break the pawn structure while you have the initiative, instead of waiting.

Takeaway: In pawn endings, look for a timely pawn break to create passed pawns before improving your king.

I'd play Nf4. 

While Qc2 tries to put pressure on the queenside, it unfortunately places your queen on the same file as Black's rook, which can lead to discovered attacks later. Moving the knight to f4 is much more active—it improves your passive piece on e2, opens up the e-file for your rook, and eyes important central squares like d5. This maneuver helps you fight for the center instead of tiptoeing around Black's strong knight on c4.

Takeaway: Always look to improve your least active pieces and avoid lining up your queen with enemy rooks.

I'd play Kd4. This is a tricky pawn endgame where you're going to be down a pawn either way, so the key is activity, not clinging to the b4-pawn. By playing Kc3 you stayed passive and let Black's king roam free with Ka4; instead, Kd4 grabs the d5-pawn's attention, defends your strong e5-pawn, and keeps your king in the center where it does the most work. Yes, Black plays Kxb4, but after Kd3 your active king and central pawns give you real counterplay against Black's e6- and d5-pawns.

Takeaway: In king-and-pawn endgames, an active, centralized king is worth more than a pawn you can't hold anyway.

I'd play Rg6. This is actually the right idea: move the rook out of the bishop’s attack while keeping it protected by your king, so Black’s king cannot take it. The concrete plan is simple: keep your king and rook working together, then look to win Black’s loose rook on h3 or push your g-pawn when it is safe.  
Takeaway: In rook endgames, put your rook where your king protects it and the enemy king cannot capture it.

I'd play h5. This is a more active beginner-friendly move: it attacks the knight on g6 right away, instead of using the rook move to defend pieces. Your Re1 did defend the queen on e4 and bishop on f1, but here you can gain time by making Black answer a direct threat. If Black plays a6, you can continue with Nd4 and keep improving your pieces.

Takeaway: When you can attack an enemy piece safely, prefer gaining time over making a purely defensive move.

I'd play Nf4. Qc2 is a natural-looking move, but it doesn’t really challenge Black’s active pieces or create pressure; Nf4 improves your quiet knight and immediately puts pressure on d5 and e6. Your plan is simple: keep building on those central targets, and if Black tries ...g5, Bg3 keeps the bishop safe while your knight stays active.  
Takeaway: When the position is tense, improve your least active piece with a move that also attacks something.

I'd play Kd2. 

Trying to hold onto the b4 pawn with Kc3 backfires because Black simply plays Ka4, outflanking you and marching right into your position. By playing Kd2, you accept that the b4 pawn is lost, but after Black takes it with Kxb4, you reply with Kd3. This claims the opposition and acts as a wall, keeping the Black king from advancing further into your territory and maximizing your practical defensive chances.

Takeaway: In king and pawn endgames, preventing the enemy king from infiltrating is often much more important than clinging to a doomed pawn.

I'd play Nxc7+. You had a great chance to win material with a knight fork, but Re1 let the moment slip. From b5 your knight can jump to c7, giving check to the king on e8 and attacking the rook in the corner on a8 at the same time — the king must move and then you scoop up the rook. Always look for checks that also hit something else, because your opponent can only deal with one threat at a time.

Takeaway: Before making a quiet move, scan for checks that fork the king and another piece.

I'd play Kd4.

By playing Kc3, you keep everything solid, but in this pawn endgame, you need to find a way to break through and create a passed pawn. Sacrificing the b4 pawn with Kd4 is the winning breakthrough! If Black accepts the sacrifice with ...Kxb4, your king infiltrates with Kxd5, guaranteeing you win the e6 pawn next and clear the path for your e5 pawn to march to promotion. 

Takeaway: In pawn endgames, don't be afraid to sacrifice a pawn to create a decisive king infiltration route.

I'd play h5. Your move Re1 was safe and defended your pieces, but it was passive and let Black off the hook — when you have the initiative, look for a move that makes a threat instead of just tidying up. Here h5 attacks the knight on g6 and forces Black to react to you, keeping you on the front foot. If Black tries to grab space with a6, you can even reroute your knight toward the center with Nd4, improving your position while Black is busy defending.

Takeaway: When your pieces are active, prefer a move that creates a threat over a move that just quietly defends.

I'd play Qxc1#. Your move Nbc6 developed a piece, but this position has something much more urgent: the white rook on c1 is undefended, and your queen can capture it with checkmate. When you have a forcing move that ends the game, take it before making a normal developing move.

Takeaway: Always check for forcing captures and checks before choosing a routine developing move.

I'd play h5. 

Your move, Re1, is a bit too passive and misses a chance to take control. By pushing your pawn to h5, you immediately attack Black's knight on g6 and force it to move. I'd play Qxa2. Your Nbc6 is a fine developing move, but it ignores that your queen already sits deep in White's camp with real work to do — grabbing the a2-pawn keeps material even and stays active. I'd play Qxc1#. When you played Nbc6, you developed a piece but overlooked that the game was already yours; before making any quiet developing move, always scan whether your queen or rooks are lining up against the enemy king. The clue here was the undefended rook next to the king along an open line your queen controls.

Takeaway: Before a routine move, always check if you have a forcing capture or check — winning material or the game beats developing.

I'd play Nxc7+.

It is very natural to try and bring your rook to an active square like you did with your move, but you missed a fantastic tactical opportunity! Your knight on b5 and your queen on e5 are both attacking the pawn on c7, while Black only has their queen on b6 defending it. Because you have two attackers and Black only has one defender, you can safely play Nxc7+ to either win Black's queen or fork their king and rook.

Takeaway: Always count your attackers and defenders on weak points near the enemy king—you might just have enough pieces to crash through!

I'd play Kd4. This is the active king move: instead of using your king just to babysit the b-pawn with Kc3, you step into the center and challenge Black’s d5/e6 structure directly. In this kind of pawn ending, the b-pawn is less important than winning the race for key central squares; if Black goes pawn-hunting, your king and kingside pawns get the first chance to create a passed pawn.

I'd play d6.

Whenever you can kick a powerful enemy piece and improve your own position at the same time, it's usually the right choice.

I'd play Bxd2+. If White’s king takes, Qb2+ becomes a nasty follow-up that lines up with the queen on e5, so your plan is check first, then attack the loose queen. Takeaway: When you can make a forcing move with check before attacking a queen, look at that move first.

I'd play Qxc1#.

I'd play Ne2. This is a calm developing move that also fixes two practical issues: it defends your queen on c1 and your pawn on g3. Compared with d4, it improves your worst-placed piece first and keeps your position more flexible; a simple plan is Ne2, and if Black plays Nf6, continue Bc3.

I'd play exd4. I'd play Ne2. Takeaway: When you're under pressure, prefer a developing move that also patches your loose squares over a pawn advance that resolves nothing.

I'd play Be3. Your Kh1 move spent a tempo on safety, but nothing concrete needed fixing there; Be3 develops with purpose by attacking the bishop on c5 and also defending the pawn on f2. If Black trades with Bxe3, you can recapture with Rxe3 and keep improving your pieces instead of drifting.

I'd play Ne2.

I'd play b4. Your Kh1 is safe but passive — it doesn't do anything active, and in this position you have a great chance to gain space and chase Black's pieces. I'd play Be3. The move Kh1 didn't do anything useful — it just shuffled the king while ignoring the active black bishop sitting on c5. I'd play Be3. This improves a piece while making Black answer your threat to trade off the active bishop on c5; if Black plays Bxe3, Rxe3 leaves your rook more active. I'd play Na4.

I'd play b4.

I'd play Qxd8. Before grabbing the pawn, trade queens so your rook on d1 is defended and Black’s most active piece is gone. After Qxd8 Bxd8, then fxe4 becomes a much cleaner plan because you have reduced the pressure first.

I'd play fxe4. Your capture was actually the right idea! I'd play Qc3. Your move fxe4 rushed to grab material, but it let the queens come off on your terms only after helping Black — the real problem was opening things up before improving your pieces. I'd play Nxc7+. This is the forcing version of your idea: the knight captures the pawn on c7 with check and attacks the rook on a8, so Black has to answer your check before dealing with the rook. I'd play Bxf6.

I'd play Qc3.

I'd play Nf4+. Your Ng6 grabbed at activity but let Black off the hook — the key here is that his king is exposed in the center and you want to strike with tempo before he consolidates. With Nf4+ you check the king and, more importantly, threaten to win material while keeping your pieces coordinated; after the king moves you can follow up hitting the loose bishop on c2 and the knight on d4. Takeaway: When the opponent's king is exposed, look for checks that gain tempo and set up threats, not just moves that "look active."

I'd play Nxc7+. Your Ng6 was trying to save the stranded knight on h8, but it hands Black a free tempo — after ...hxg6 you've traded your active plan for a passive one. I'd play Nxc7+. Your Ng6 moved the edge knight, but the urgent feature is the fork already sitting on d5: Nxc7+ checks the exposed king and attacks the rook on a8. After something like Nxc7+ Kd6 Nxa8, you’ve turned your active knight into material instead of letting Black regroup. I'd play Qxd8. Before grabbing the e-pawn, remove Black’s queen and simplify the heavy-piece tension on the d-file; after Qxd8 Raxd8 2.Rxd8+ Rxd8, you’ve traded into a much calmer position. I'd play Be3. This develops a bishop and makes your queen on d4 safer, instead of spending a move grabbing the d6 pawn with the queen. For now, aim to bring pieces out and support your queen before taking loose pawns.  
Takeaway: In the opening, develop and stabilize your pieces before going pawn-hunting with the queen.

I'd play Qd3. Instead of only moving the rook away, this move makes an active threat: your queen attacks the annoying bishop on b3, so Black has to deal with it. If Black grabs with Bxd1, you can recapture with Kxd1, and you have at least removed that dangerous bishop rather than letting it sit there and keep causing problems. I'd play Nxc7+.

By jumping to c7 with your d5 knight, you deliver a devastating fork on the black king and the rook on a8. Sometimes, letting a doomed piece go is the best choice when you can win far more material on the other side of the board.

I'd play Qxd6. Grabbing the pawn here is actually the right call, and it's completely safe — after the trades on d4 there's nothing defending that d6 pawn, so you win it cleanly. Takeaway: When a pawn is truly undefended and you see no real threat, take it — free material adds up.

I'd play Be3. Grabbing the d6-pawn with Qxd6 looks tempting, but it drags your queen into the open where Black can hit it with checks and gain time chasing it around — you win a pawn but hand over the initiative. Instead, Be3 quietly develops a piece and, importantly, gives your queen a solid friend on d4 so she can't be bullied. I'd play Nxc7+. 

While trying to save your trapped knight on the kingside with Ng6 feels urgent, you missed a golden opportunity on the other side of the board. By playing the knight fork on c7, you immediately win a critical pawn and the rook on a8, gaining a massive material advantage that far outweighs the trapped piece. When you have a powerful, centralized piece like the knight on d5, always scan for tactical targets in the enemy camp before worrying about your awkwardly placed pieces.

Takeaway: Before spending time trying to rescue a stranded piece, check if you have a forcing tactical sequence that wins even more material.

I'd play Be2. Your Qxd6 grabs a pawn, but it sends the queen into enemy territory while your king is still in the center and your pieces are undeveloped. Be2 fixes the real priority: get ready to castle, connect your pieces, and only then look for pawn wins with a safer king.

I'd play Rxh6+. Instead of drifting the rook back with Rh8, grab the free pawn with check — you win material immediately and stay active. After the king moves, you'll swing your rook down to grab the g4-pawn too, cleaning up Black's counterplay and steering toward an easy winning endgame. When you're ahead in an endgame like this, the plan is to munch weak pawns while keeping your rook on active files and ranks, not to shuffle it to a passive square.

Takeaway: In rook endgames, look first for checks that snatch loose pawns before retreating your pieces.

I'd play Rc7. Your Rh8 move does attack the pawn on h6, but Rc7 is more instructive because it keeps the rook active and ready to check from the side. A simple plan is Rc7, and if the black king goes to g5, you have Rc5+.

I'd play Bc4.

I'd play Be3.

I'd play Rc7. Your move Rh8 grabbed at the h6 pawn, but this position is about your rook staying active and getting behind or beside the enemy pawn, not chasing a single pawn into the corner. I'd play d5. I'd play a5. It attacks the pawn on b4, just like your c5 idea, but it does it in the most direct and easy-to-understand way. I'd play a5. Your idea of pushing on the queenside was actually right — the problem with c5 is that it lets White play in a way that leaves your pawns a bit loose. Instead, a5 hits the b4 pawn directly and asks White a question without weakening your own structure, and you keep your good bishop on b6 pointing at White's kingside. I'd play f5. I'd play Rc7.

I'd play Ra7.

I'd play axb5. Your check with f3+ is tempting, but in this pawn ending the most important thing is the queenside structure: axb5 removes White’s advanced pawn and attacks the pawn on a4. I'd play a6. Your move c5 is a bit committal and allows White to push their pawn to b5, locking down the queenside and restricting your pieces. I'd play a5.

I'd play axb5. This is a race, and the key is to see whose passed pawn queens first — your b5-pawn is closer to promoting than White's, so grabbing it keeps your fastest runner alive. Playing f3+ just chases the king toward your pawns for free and loses time; after axb5 you have a real threat of pushing b5-b4-b3 while White is tied up. Count the tempos rather than reaching for a check that accomplishes nothing.

Takeaway: In pawn races, don't waste moves on aimless checks — push and support your fastest passed pawn.

I'd play axb5. I'd play Nxc2+. Instead of castling, use the forcing move first: your knight gives check and also attacks the rook on a1. This forces Black onto the defensive, completely shifting the momentum in your favor while keeping your powerful passed pawn alive.

I'd play Ke3. This keeps your king close to the pawn and directly defends it, which is the key job in this position. Your concrete plan is simple: keep the pawn safe and look to play e1=Q when it is ready.  
Takeaway: In king-and-pawn endings, support your passed pawn with the king before trying to cash it in.

I'd play e1=Q. You already have a passed pawn just one step from the finish line, so the most important thing is to promote it and add a powerful new queen to the fight. When you played Kf2, you left the pawn sitting there and gave your opponent time to organize against it — always cash in a pawn that's ready to queen rather than shuffling your king around it. With a fresh queen on the board, you'll be the one making threats instead of nervously defending.

Takeaway: When your pawn reaches the last rank, promote it — a new queen is almost always more valuable than any waiting move.

I'd play Ke3. Your instinct to protect the pawn on e2 was exactly right — a passed pawn one step from promotion is your winning ticket, so you don't want to let it fall. The problem with Kf2 is that it steps too far to the side; Ke3 stays right next to your pawn while keeping your king closer to helping it queen, so you can push e1=Q with your king shielding the way. Guard the pawn, then shepherd it home.

Takeaway: When you have a pawn one square from queening, keep your king close beside it to protect and escort it in.

I'd play Ke3. Your instinct to move your king was spot on, but bringing it to e3 securely defends your advanced pawn while keeping your king active. By stepping closer to the center while protecting your most important asset, you guarantee that White cannot stop you from promoting to a queen on the next turn. 

Takeaway: In the endgame, use your king actively to escort your passed pawns safely to promotion.

I'd play Kf4. 

While moving closer to the promotion square seems logical, your move walks right into the sightlines of White's knight. By playing your king to f4 instead, you support your passed pawn while staying safely out of range of any sudden knight forks that could win your pawn. It's crucial to keep your most important asset safe while figuring out how to push it through.

Takeaway: When nursing a passed pawn to promotion, always double-check that your king's path doesn't walk into a knight fork.

I'd play Kxd8. Your Kd7 move left the white bishop on d8 alive, but Kxd8 simply removes it and also protects the c7 pawn. After that, Black has solved the immediate problem and can continue calmly with the extra material. This is the kind of forcing move you should look for first: if an enemy piece is sitting next to your king and can be safely taken, take it.

Takeaway: When you can safely capture an undefended piece and improve your position, do that before making a quiet king move.

I'd play Ra8+. Start with the forcing check before pushing the passer: after Kb7, Rf8 puts the rook behind the f-pawn and keeps pressure on Black’s kingside while your e-pawn is still ready to advance. Your e6 move has the right idea, but it gives Black a tempo to start counterplay instead of making the king move first. Takeaway: When you have a strong passed pawn, look for useful checks that improve your pieces before committing the pawn.

I'd play Kxd8. Your king is in check from the rook on e1, but instead of just running to d7, you can solve the check and pick up the loose bishop on d8 at the same time. Takeaway: When you are in check, don’t only ask “Where can I move?”—also ask “Can I get something useful while escaping?”

I'd play Kxd8. There's a free bishop sitting right next to your king on d8, so grab it — capturing it also keeps your c7 pawn protected, which is a nice bonus. When you played Kd7 you stepped away and left that bishop alive, missing an easy chance to win a whole piece for nothing. Whenever an enemy piece is hanging next to your king and nothing bad happens after you take it, just take it.

Takeaway: Before moving elsewhere, always check whether you can simply capture an undefended enemy piece.

I'd play Kxd8. That bishop sitting on d8 is giving check and hanging for free, so the simplest and strongest move is just to capture it with your king. Your Kd7 walked away from the check without addressing it and left you down material; grabbing the bishop wins a whole piece and untangles your king at the same time. Always check what's actually attacking you before you decide where to move.

Takeaway: When you're in check, look first for the move that captures the checking piece — especially when it's undefended.

I'd play Rxf5. Your Kf6 was understandable because it attacks the knight and defends g5, but Rxf5 is cleaner: remove the knight immediately and also keep g5 protected. If White recaptures with Kxf5, Black can continue with b5, using the queenside pawn as the next concrete plan.

I'd play Kxd8. 

Moving the king to d7 avoids some danger, but it misses a golden opportunity to simply grab White's unprotected bishop. By taking with your king on d8, you eliminate a dangerous enemy piece from your territory and simultaneously defend your c7 pawn. Don't be afraid to use your king to capture hanging pieces when there is no immediate mating net.

Takeaway: When your opponent drops an unprotected piece deep into your position, capturing it is usually the safest and best response.

I'd play Kf8. 

Moving your king to d7 looks natural to get out of the check, but it steps right into a devastating knight fork. White would play Ne5+, checking your king and winning your queen on g6. I'd play Be8. Your Bc3 had the right idea of noticing the dangerous a-pawn, but it is too passive and lets Black keep bossing your bishop around with the rook. I'd play Kf7. Takeaway: Before advancing your king in an endgame, check that it isn't stepping into a knight fork or check that loses precious tempo.

I'd play Rxf5. You're already winning here with your extra rook, and the cleanest path is to trade it for the annoying knight so nothing can distract you from your real winner: the passed b-pawn. I'd play Bd7. This develops your bishop and, importantly, adds protection to both the b5 pawn and the e6 pawn. After White’s next move, your plan is to finish coordinating your heavy pieces and only then look for pawn breaks.

I'd play Ke3. Your move Kf2 rushes to support the promotion, but it lets White give up the rook for your new queen and leaves you with no pawn left. I'd play Rxf5.

I'd play Bd7. I'd play Kf7.

I'd play b6.

I'd play Bd7.

Takeaway: Always prioritize finishing your development and activating your pieces before making pawn moves on the flank.

I'd play Bd7. Your move is actually the right idea! You noticed that White's bishop on a4 is aiming at a hidden target: your rook on a8 is undefended and lined up with that bishop. I'd play Rc7. This puts your rook directly in front of White’s dangerous passed c-pawn and also attacks it, which is much more urgent than playing a slow move like a5. If White tries c6, you can simply meet it with Rxc6, so your plan is clear: blockade first, then win the pawn.

I'd play Ke7. The move you chose develops a piece, but here Black’s priority is to make the king useful and stabilize the position. Ke7 directly defends the queen on f6 and the pawn on f7, so you are not leaving important points loose while you continue improving. I'd play Ke7. I'd play Ke7.

I'd play Kf8. Your Bd7 stops the check, but it also lets White trade off your bishop and helps them simplify; Kf8 calmly steps out of check while keeping your bishop available. I'd play Kf8.

I'd play Ke6. I'd play gxf6. Capturing away from the center with the g-pawn keeps your queen home and, crucially, opens the g-file for your rook toward White's king — after Qxf6 your queen sits on an exposed square where White gains time by hitting it or trading into a position where your extra structure means less. I'd play g6. I'd play Bxd5. By capturing that knight with your bishop, you trade off White's most active piece and relieve the pressure on your cramped position before finishing your development and castling. Simple, healthy trades like this keep you safe when your own pieces aren't yet coordinated.

I'd play gxf6.

Capturing with the pawn instead opens up the g-file for your rook and keeps your queen safely tucked away until your development is completed.

I'd play Nxd5. The key is to take the undefended knight on d5 instead of moving your knight away with Nc2. I'd play g6.

I'd play Nxd5. Your knight on b4 went hunting on c2, but that's off on the edge chasing nothing while an enemy knight sits right in the middle of your position. Takeaway: Before looking for fancy jumps, check whether you can simply capture an undefended enemy piece.

I'd play Nxd5.

I'd play Re8. This is a more purposeful move than Nbd7 because it immediately puts pressure on the pawn on e4 while also defending the queen on d8. Your plan is simple and reusable: improve the rook first, then look for d5 to challenge the center and attack the bishop on c4.

I'd play O-O.

I'd play Bxf3. I'd play d5. After the bishop steps back you can follow up with a move like Re8 to pile onto the center and keep the initiative.

I'd play d5. When White’s king is still stuck in the center, you usually want to open or challenge the center instead of making a quiet developing move like Nbd7. After d5, you hit the bishop on c4 and the pawn on e4, and if exd5 cxd5, Black gets a strong central pawn and more active pieces. Takeaway: When the opponent’s king is uncastled, look for a central break before making a routine developing move.

I'd play Nxd5. That knight on d5 is White’s most annoying piece, attacking your bishop and important squares, so trading it off is the clean, practical move. Your Nc2 move goes pawn-hunting/rook-hunting while leaving White’s best piece in the center; Nxd5 fixes the real problem first, and if White recaptures with a bishop, your queen can recapture. Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong centralized piece, look to trade it off before chasing side targets.

I'd play Bf5. Your Bd7 was playable, but it focused on defending the rook on e8; Bf5 is more active because it moves the bishop while also attacking the pawn on h7. A clear plan is Bf5, and if White plays fxe5, then dxe5 keeps Black’s center organized while the bishop stays active.  
Takeaway: When you can improve a piece and create a target at the same time, prefer that over a purely defensive move.

I'd play Nf7. This calmly asks White’s active bishop what it is doing before you spend the queen on the f6-pawn; if White takes, Kxf7 trades off an attacker and the f6-pawn is much easier to collect or neutralize. Compared with Qxf6, this is more disciplined: improve a piece, reduce White’s attacking force, then clean up the advanced pawn.

I'd play Bxf3. 

While developing your knight with your move is a very natural instinct, trading on f3 creates immediate, permanent problems for White. Because White's king is still stuck in the center, forcing them to recapture with the pawn (shattering their kingside structure) or the Queen (misplacing it) takes advantage of their lack of development. Whenever your opponent delays castling, look for active trades or pawn breaks that make their king's life uncomfortable before you settle into quiet development.

Takeaway: If your opponent's king is stuck in the center, look for ways to create structural damage or open lines before they can castle to safety.

I'd play d5.

Your move Nbd7 is a normal developing move, but it is a bit slow and misses a chance to seize the initiative. Because White's king is still sitting in the middle of the board and their queen is somewhat exposed on the e-file, you want to blow the center open as quickly as possible. Pushing your pawn to d5 immediately attacks the bishop on c4 and the pawn on e4, forcing White to react to your threats while their king is still vulnerable.

Takeaway: Always look for aggressive central pawn breaks when your opponent delays castling.

I'd play exf4. By taking on f4 you open the e-file for your rook and queen battery, activate your pieces toward White's king, and remove a pawn that supports the knight on g5 — suddenly you're the one attacking instead of shuffling backward. Grabbing the initiative and using your well-placed pieces beats tidying up when the position is sharp.

Takeaway: In tense positions, look to open lines for your active pieces before quietly retreating.

I'd play Bf5. Your bishop on g4 was hanging in the air with nothing to do, and Bd7 solved the safety issue but tucked the piece away passively behind your own position. Bf5 keeps the bishop active and immediately hits the pawn on h7, giving your piece a job instead of just a hiding spot; after the likely fxe5 dxe5 your pieces coordinate much more naturally. The key difference is that when you retreat a threatened piece, you want it landing on a square where it still influences the game.

Takeaway: When a piece is attacked, look for a retreat that keeps it doing work rather than one that just gets it out of danger.

I'd play Nf5. Your instinct here is actually great, so let's keep it! By jumping into f5 you attack Black's bishop on g6, and you also threaten the fork on g7 that would hit both the king and the rook on h8. This is the kind of active, forcing move that puts your opponent on the back foot and forces them to react to you instead of the other way around.

Takeaway: A knight that lands deep in the enemy camp attacking two things at once is usually a strong, forcing move.

I'd play Bf5. 

While moving the bishop to d7 is perfectly safe, it places the piece on a rather passive square. By playing Bf5 instead, you immediately put pressure on White's deeply advanced and annoying h7 pawn. This active placement also controls a critical diagonal, preparing you to handle the tension in the center when White captures on e5. 

Takeaway: When retreating or repositioning a piece, always look for the most active square that addresses your opponent's most dangerous ideas.

I'd play Qf3. Your Nf5 move was a bit rushed; Qf3 is calmer and more useful because it attacks the knight on f6 while also defending your pawns on d3 and e4. That gives you a simple plan: support your center first, then look for active moves.  
Takeaway: Before jumping forward, make sure your key pawns and pieces are supported.

I'd play Qf3. Your knight jump to f5 sent your piece charging forward, but it wasn't defended by anything and had no lasting job there — pieces do their best work when they're both active and safe. Instead, Qf3 develops your queen to a strong central spot where it presses against the knight on f6 while also guarding your pawns on d3 and e4, keeping your whole position solid. This is the kind of quiet, useful move that improves your army without hanging anything.

Takeaway: Before pushing a piece deep into enemy territory, ask whether it's actually safe and doing a real job.

I'd play exf4. 

By capturing on f4, you actively challenge White's space and immediately open up the e-file for your rooks to create counterplay. Retreating the bishop with Bd7 is simply too passive; it gives White a free hand to maintain the tension or push forward with f5 to build their attack. In a sharp position where White has a dangerous passed pawn on h7, you cannot afford to shuffle pieces backward without fighting back in the center.

Takeaway: When facing a dangerous attack on the flank, active central counterplay is often your most reliable defense.

I'd play Nxg6. Your knight on h4 is attacking Black’s bishop, so take that bishop instead of jumping to f5 where the bishop can simply capture you. After Nxg6, if Black recaptures, you have traded your knight for an active bishop and weakened Black’s kingside; then your plan is to develop calmly and use the open lines around Black’s uncastled king.  
Takeaway: When your piece attacks a valuable piece, consider taking it before moving away to a square where it can be captured.

I'd play Rxf4. This is much more active than Bd7: instead of moving the bishop away, you remove the f-pawn that was guarding the knight on g5. If White accepts with Rxf4, your e-pawn recaptures; if White tries Qxg4, then Rxg4 wins the queen, so your plan is simply to clear the defender and then take on g5.

I'd play Nxg6.

Your move, Nf5, puts the knight on an active square, but Black can simply capture it with their bishop, trading away your well-placed piece. Instead, capturing the bishop right away with Nxg6 is a fantastic and reliable plan. It permanently wins the "bishop pair" for you, and when Black recaptures with their pawn, their pawn structure will be left doubled and slightly weakened.

Takeaway: When your knight is attacking an enemy bishop, capturing it to damage their pawn structure and secure the bishop pair is usually a great long-term strategy.

I'd play cxd3. Your move b6 is understandable — you're trying to keep the queenside solid — but it ignores the most urgent feature of the position: White's queen is stuck deep on a4 attacking your bishop, and you can grab material and open lines with cxd3. After cxd3 Bxd3, your bishop on a5 is safe and you've won a clean pawn while activating your position; meanwhile b6 leaves that tension unresolved and lets White choose the favorable moment to act. Always check whether you can just take before you make a quiet defensive move.

Takeaway: When a capture is available, calculate it first — quiet moves can wait, but free material and open lines usually shouldn't.

I'd play d4. Your center pawns are your biggest asset here, so instead of spending a move on b6, push forward and challenge White's pawn chain — d4 hits both the c3 and e3 pawns at once, forcing White to react to you rather than the other way around. This opens lines for your pieces and keeps the initiative firmly in your hands, whereas b6 was a slow move that didn't develop anything or create a threat. After this you can follow up naturally with e4 and get your king castled.

Takeaway: When you have a strong pawn center, use it to strike and make threats rather than playing slow, passive pawn moves.

I'd play Qf3. 

Jumping your knight into f5 looks tempting, but it is a bit premature because your position isn't fully developed yet. Instead, bringing your queen to f3 is a great, solid improving move.
