Takeaway: In the endgame, always look to centralize your king and use it to attack enemy pieces and support your own pawns.

I'd play Nc3. 

Playing Kg8 is a passive waiting move that allows White to improve their setup without a fight. Instead, playing your knight to c3 actively improves your piece by attacking the White rook with tempo. Forcing the rook to move gives you time to push your b-pawn or reposition the knight to a more dominant square, keeping the pressure on White.

Takeaway: In endgames, prioritize improving your pieces with tempo over making passive king moves.

I'd play Ke6. In this endgame your king is a fighting piece, and Ke6 improves it with tempo by attacking the bishop instead of drifting back with Kg8. After the bishop moves, you can keep centralizing and use the king to support the b-pawn and slow White’s kingside pawns.  
Takeaway: In endgames, prefer active king moves that gain time over passive retreats.

I'd play Ke3. In this rook ending, the check with Rf5+ is tempting, but Ke3 improves your position immediately by defending both d3 and e4. After that, your plan is simple and useful: bring the rook to c4, where it attacks b4 while also helping defend c2 and e4.  
Takeaway: In rook endings, prioritize improving your king and securing your pawns before giving checks.

I'd play Rc4. Your Rf5+ was a natural-looking check, but it just chases the king to a good square and doesn't improve your position — a common trap when you reach for a check on autopilot. Rc4 does real work: it attacks the loose b4-pawn while staying active, and after something like Rb7 you can follow with Ke3 to bring your king up and shore up your center. In a defensive rook ending, activity plus targeting the opponent's weakest pawn matters far more than a tempo-losing check.

Takeaway: In rook endings, don't check for its own sake — put your rook where it attacks a weakness and your king where it defends.

I'd play Kc2. In this rook endgame your king is under fire from Black's rook on h1, so before doing anything active you want to tuck the king to safety and end the checks — Kc2 does that and keeps everything defended. Rf5+ feels active, but it's a check that goes nowhere: after Black's king simply steps away you've gained nothing and your own king is still exposed on the back rank. Once you're safe on c2, your extra pawns and centralized rook give you a genuine pull, and you can start pushing your central and queenside majority.

Takeaway: In rook endgames, safeguard your own king before chasing checks — a check that improves nothing just loses a tempo.

I'd play Bxb6. Your Nd2 move steps into the path of your own bishop and gives up the pressure you already had on the knight; Bxb6 cashes in that pressure right away. If Black recaptures with ...Qxb6, you can follow with Nxe5, improving your knight and picking off the central pawn instead of retreating passively.  
Takeaway: When one of your pieces is creating pressure, look for a way to use that pressure before making a defensive retreat.

I'd play O-O. Your Qb3 move brings the queen out, but it leaves your king in the center when you have a simple chance to make it safe. Castling also connects your plan to defense: it defends the rook on f1 and the pawn on f2, so your position becomes easier to play. After O-O, you can develop calmly instead of trying to create queen pressure too early.

Takeaway: In the opening, make your king safe before starting queen adventures.

I'd play Ke3.

I'd play cxd5. Takeaway: Before making threats, check whether you can simply capture an undefended or attacked piece.

I'd play O-O. Your move Qb3 sent the queen out early looking for action, but there was still important business at home: your king is stuck in the center with all your rooks disconnected. I'd play Rc8+.

I'd play Nc4. This improves a piece with tempo: the knight attacks the queen on a5 and also eyes the pawn on e5, while helping defend your a3 and b2 pawns. I'd play Nc4. I'd play Nc4. This develops a piece with tempo by attacking the queen, and the knight lands on a strong square where it also eyes the e5 pawn. I'd play Qe8. I'd play Ke8. This uses your king actively to hit the rook and force it off the strong seventh rank, instead of spending a tempo on a queenside pawn move while White’s rook stays active. I'd play Qg4. Your Rg3 move stacked the rook behind the queen, but it left the rook doing very little and weakened your coordination without creating any real threat. I'd play Qg4.

I'd play f5.

I'd play Nh5. Your bishop on d6 was actually being eyed by the bishop on f4, and instead of defending, Be6 walked into trouble and gave away material. I'd play Nh5.

I'd play Nh5.

I'd play Bb5. Your Bxe5 gives up the pressure too quickly; Bb5 keeps the bishop active and immediately attacks the rook on e8. I'd play Bxe5. Your move is actually the strongest here, so trust your instinct — but let's understand exactly why it works. I'd play Nh5. This is more forcing than Be6 because it attacks the queen on g3 while also putting pressure on the bishop on f4, so White cannot calmly improve or trade pieces. I'd play Ke4. This is a very beginner-friendly improvement: your king steps into the center and defends both the bishop on d5 and the pawn on f5. I'd play c6. Your pawn push is good because it turns the c-pawn into a real promotion threat, and if Black grabs it with Rxc6, your bishop can recapture with Bxc6.
